revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = <!-- descriptive text for use by speech synthesis (text-to-speech) software --> | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = <!-- only use if different from name --> | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[Japanese nationalism]] * [[Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons category|Kamo no Mabuchi}} {{Authority control}}

His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[Japanese nationalism]] * [[Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons category|Kamo no Mabuchi}} {{Authority control}}
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = <!-- descriptive text for use by speech synthesis (text-to-speech) software --> | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = <!-- only use if different from name --> | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[Japanese nationalism]] * [[Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons category|Kamo no Mabuchi}} {{Authority control}}

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = <!-- descriptive text for use by speech synthesis (text-to-speech) software --> | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = <!-- only use if different from name --> | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[Japanese nationalism]] * [[Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons category|Kamo no Mabuchi}} {{Authority control}}
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = <!-- descriptive text for use by speech synthesis (text-to-speech) software --> | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = <!-- only use if different from name --> | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[Japanese nationalism]] * [[Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Kamo no Mabuchi]]

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = <!-- descriptive text for use by speech synthesis (text-to-speech) software --> | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = <!-- only use if different from name --> | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = <!-- descriptive text for use by speech synthesis (text-to-speech) software --> | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = <!-- only use if different from name --> | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[Japanese nationalism]] * [[Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Kamo no Mabuchi]]

In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].

In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = <!-- descriptive text for use by speech synthesis (text-to-speech) software --> | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = <!-- only use if different from name --> | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[Japanese nationalism]] * [[Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Kamo no Mabuchi]]

Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].

Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

Along with [[Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[:en:Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]], [[Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[:en:Japanese language|Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[:en:Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[Nihonbashi]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[:en:Japanese nationalism|Japanese nationalism]] * [[:en:Keichū|Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}}

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = | birth_date = {{Birth date|1697|04|24}} | birth_place = [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | death_date = {{Death date and age|1769|11|27|1697|04|24}} | death_place = [[:en:Edo|Edo]], Japan | nationality = Japanese | other_names = | occupation = | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works = }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]

{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[poet]] and [[philologist]] during mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]], [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[:en:Lyman's Law|Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[rendaku]]'' in the [[:en:Japanese language|Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[:en:Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[:en:Nihonbashi|Nihonbashi]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[:en:Japanese nationalism|Japanese nationalism]] * [[:en:Keichū|Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] * [[Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}}

{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[:en:Poet|Poet]] and [[:en:philologist|philologist]] during mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]], [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[:en:Lyman's Law|Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[:en:Rendaku|Rendaku]]'' in the [[:en:Japanese language|Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[:en:Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[:en:Nihonbashi|Nihonbashi]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[:en:Japanese nationalism|Japanese nationalism]] * [[:en:Keichū|Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] * [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}}

{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[:en:Poet|Poet]] and [[:en:philologist|philologist]] during mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]], [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[:en:Lyman's Law|Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[:en:Rendaku|Rendaku]]'' in the [[:en:Japanese language|Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[:en:Tokugawa Munetake|Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[:en:Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[:en:Tale of Genji|Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[:en:Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[:en:Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[:en:Nihonbashi|Nihonbashi]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[:en:Japanese nationalism|Japanese nationalism]] * [[:en:Keichū|Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] * [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}}

}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1343397]] Wikidata name: Kamo no Mabuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1343397]] Wikidata name: Kamo no Mabuchi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | name = Kamo no Mabuchi | image = File:Kamo no Mabuchi.jpg | alt = | caption = Mabuchi's portrait by his student | birth_name = | birth_date = {{birth date|1697|4|24}} | birth_place = {{ill|Iba|qid=Q11379268}} | death_date = {{death date|1769|11|27}} | death_place = {{ill|Edo|qid=Q215646}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}} | other_names = | occupation ={{ill|linguist|qid=Q14467526}}; {{ill|philosopher|qid=Q4964182}}; {{ill|poet|qid=Q49757}}; {{ill|writer|qid=Q36180}} | years_active = | known_for = | notable_works ={{ill|Q11274839|qid=Q11274839}}; {{ill|Q11420930|qid=Q11420930}}; {{ill|Q11564176|qid=Q11564176}} | gender= {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | organization= {{ill|Four Great Men of Kokugaku|qid=Q29915315}} | native_name= 賀茂 真淵 | resting_place= {{ill|Tōkai-ji|qid=Q11527865}} }} [[Image:Kamo no Mabuchi in Hamamatsu.JPG|thumb|220px|Bust of Mabuchi in Hamamatsu]]
{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[:en:Poet|Poet]] and [[:en:philologist|philologist]] during mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]], [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[:en:Lyman's Law|Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[:en:Rendaku|Rendaku]]'' in the [[:en:Japanese language|Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[:en:Tokugawa Munetake|Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[:en:Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[:en:Tale of Genji|Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[:en:Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[:en:Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[:en:Nihonbashi|Nihonbashi]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[:en:Japanese nationalism|Japanese nationalism]] * [[:en:Keichū|Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] * [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}}

{{nihongo|'''Kamo no Mabuchi''', or '''Mabuchi of Kamo'''|賀茂 真淵||24 April 1697 – 27 November 1769}} was a ''[[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]]'' scholar, [[:en:Poet|Poet]] and [[:en:philologist|philologist]] during mid-[[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] Japan. Along with [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]], [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], and [[Hirata Atsutane]], he was regarded as one of the [[:en:Four Great Men of Kokugaku|Four Great Men of Kokugaku]], and through his research into the spirit of ancient Japan (through his studies of the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and other works of ancient literature) he expounded on the theory of ''[[magokoro]]'', which he held to be fundamental to the history of Japan.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Olivier Ansart|url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/ebisu_1340-3656_1994_num_4_1_896|title=Études Anciennes et Études Nationales dans le Japon du XVIIIème siècle : la Nature, l'Artifice et le Mal chez Ogyû Sorai et Motoori Norinaga|date=1994|isbn=|edition=|location=|page=|pages=60, 63, 64|language=fr|trans-title=Ancient Studies and National Studies in 18th Century Japan: Nature, Artifice and Evil in Ogyû Sorai and Motoori Norinaga}}</ref> Independently of and alongside his contemporary [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Mabuchi is accredited with the initial discovery of [[:en:Lyman's Law|Lyman's Law]], governing ''[[:en:Rendaku|Rendaku]]'' in the [[:en:Japanese language|Japanese language]], though which would later be named after [[:en:Benjamin Smith Lyman|Benjamin Smith Lyman]].
==Biography== Mabuchi was born in 1697 in the village of Iba in [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]] (currently part of the city of [[:en:Hamamatsu, Shizuoka|Hamamatsu, Shizuoka]]), as the third son of Okabe Masanobu. The Okabe were hereditary ''[[kannushi]]'' of [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], but his father was from a [[:en:cadet|cadet]] branch of the clan and was a farmer. In 1707, he began training under Sugiura Kuniakira, a ''kokugaku'' scholar with a private academy in Hamamatsu and a disciple of [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]]. Mabuchi married in 1723, but his wife died the following year. At the age of 37, Mabuchi moved to Kyoto to study directly under Kada no Azumamaro. Following the master's death in 1736, Mabuchi moved to [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in 1738 where he taught ''kokugaku''. In 1746, he was hired by [[:en:Tokugawa Munetake|Tokugawa Munetake]], the head of the [[:en:Gosankyo|Tayasu branch]] of the [[Tokugawa clan]].
Mabuchi's works include commentaries on the ''Man'yōshū'', ''[[norito]]'' (Shinto prayers), ''[[kagura]]'' (Shinto dances), the ''[[:en:Tale of Genji|Tale of Genji]]'', the meaning of poems, and other ancient works and their themes. His disciples included [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]], Arakida Hisaoyu, Kato Chikage, Murata Harumi, [[Katori Nahiko]], Hanawa Hokiichi, Uchiyama Matatsu, and Kurita Hijimaro, and also included several women.
In 1763, while Mabuchi was on his way to [[:en:Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], Motoori Norinaga sought him out and became a disciple. This single night of discussions, later known as "the night in Matsuzaka", was the only occasion on which Norinaga directly received teaching from Mabuchi, although the two men later corresponded.
Mabuchi died in 1769 in Edo, at the age of 73. His grave can be found in the [[:en:Tōkai-ji (Shinagawa)|Tōkai-ji]] cemetery in [[:en:Shinagawa, Tokyo|Shinagawa]] and was designed a [[Monuments of Japan| National Historic Site]] in 1926.<ref name = "Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/171745 |title=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=4 May 2011 }}</ref> An explanatory marker stands at the site of Mabuchi's residence in Edo ([[Hisamatsu-cho]], [[:en:Nihonbashi|Nihonbashi]], [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]), and a memorial museum was built beside the house where he was born in Hamamatsu.
==See also== * [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku]] * [[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]] * [[Shinto]] * [[:en:Kada no Azumamaro|Kada no Azumamaro]] * [[:en:Japanese nationalism|Japanese nationalism]] * [[:en:Keichū|Keichū]] *[[Magokoro]] * [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] * [[:en:Ueda Akinari|Ueda Akinari]] {{commons Category|Kamo no Mabuchi}}
